data.title: Lockheed Martin: Shift In Segment Concentration Could Address Fundamental Red Flags
data.description:
Sentiment around Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) has been mixed during 2025, with the second-quarter announcement in July creating an instant panic within the investor base. Although there has been a resurgence in share price since then, the aerospace & defense giant is in need of strategic realignment in terms of its operating segments. Such realignment is needed after some negative developments in recent times. Some notable ones include interest by the U.S. government to acquire a stake in the business, losing bids on major projects to rival companies, and taking massive losses & charges on some of their ongoing programs. Beyond that, the flagship F-35 program has also gone out of favor, as the Department of Defense (DoD) has expressed concerns over the associated costs and delays related to TR-3 update.

In this analysis, I will present my bearish stance on LMT despite its cheaper valuations relative to peers. I will support my argument by throwing light on the structural issues mentioned above and related risks. I will also evaluate the company’s financial standing, key developments during the third quarter, and future prospects. So let’s begin!

Business Segments

Lockheed Martin operates through 4 business segments. As can be seen in the graph presented above, Aerospace is the largest contributor to LMT’s revenues, with a 40% contribution during the first half of 2025. This segment runs various large-scale programs, including F-35 Lightning II, C-130 Hercules, and F-16 Falcon. The importance of the aerospace segment can be emphasized through LMT’s flagship F-35 program, which contributed 30% to the company’s overall revenues in 2024.

The Missiles and Fire Control (MFC) segment develops advanced equipment and systems for manned and unmanned combat. Apart from serving the U.S. military forces and its allies, this segment also caters to the global civil nuclear power industry.

Rotary and Mission Systems (RMS) is the second largest business segment in terms of revenue contribution. Accounting for almost a quarter of the LMT’s topline, this segment operates circa 900 programs in more than 100 countries across the globe. Amongst many of its unique capabilities are the sensor and radar systems covering both land and sea.

Finally, the Space segment gives a futuristic edge to the broader business through expertise in communication, exploration, and security.

The Flagship F-35 Program

The second quarter involved important developments that raised investor uncertainty concerning the company’s F-35 program. For years, this program held an integral position within the global combat jet market, with the F-35 Lightning II being dubbed as the most lethal fifth-generation strike fighter in the world. However, the jet’s high lifetime costs, delays on technology upgrades, and issues linked with delivery and ongoing maintenance have made it a less viable option compared to more advanced alternatives.

There have been continued delays on the original timeline for the Technology Refresh 3 (TR-3), which is a software and hardware upgrade critical for the modernization of Block 4. These upgrades were originally scheduled to be finalized by April 2023, but so far, LMT has performed upgrades limited only to training use. As per the revised timeline, a series of key tests for full combat certifications is not likely to start before 2026. As per the annual Operation Test and Evaluation report, released earlier this year, the program “has shown no improvement in meeting schedule and performance timelines for developing and testing software designed to address deficiencies and add new capabilities.”

Given such uncertainties, the U.S. Department of Defense decided to curb its request for F-35 jets, both for the Air Force and the Navy. The announcement came in June and revealed that the procurement for the Air Force had been cut down by half compared to 48 jets in 2024. For the Naval forces, the request was made for only 12 jets compared to the budgeted 17. Soon after this announcement, President Trump indicated further cuts in F-35 purchases in 2026, as he wants to shift focus towards the F-47 Next Generation Air Dominance (NGAD) and Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). What is also important to note is that these developments relating to the F-35 came at the back of LMT losing the bid to Boeing (NYSE:BA) for the NGAD Program earlier this year. President Trump opted for Boeing in the allocation of a $20 billion contract for the development of the sixth-generation fighter jet, which will replace LMT’s F-22 Raptor.

Financials

Strong demand has led to revenue growth within all 4 business segments, with sales of $71.04 billion recorded in 2024. This represents a 5-year CAGR of 19% since 2019, when the total revenues were $59.81 billion. LMT registered sales of $36 billion during the first six months of 2025, compared to $35.1 billion during the same period in 2024. Therefore, the LMT management reaffirmed their sales guidance for 2025, forecasted above $74 billion.

Profitability margins have seen a dip since 2023, especially the EBITDA and net margin figures. The underlying reasons are linked with higher-than-anticipated project costs on long-term fixed-cost defense contracts. Although it is not uncommon for companies like Lockheed Martin to incur slightly higher costs on such projects, in recent periods these differences have become larger. This has also resulted in large losses on the books related to LMT’s ongoing projects, which will be discussed in further detail.

LMT’s second-quarter earnings announcement back in July sparked a panic amongst the investors. Revenues for the quarter stood at $18.2 billion, marginally missing the analysts expectations. The company did post a non-GAAP EPS of $7.29, which was an outperformance relative to the Wall Street forecasts. However, this did not induce a positive sentiment in the market because on a GAAP basis, EPS was recorded at $1.46 per share. This meant a whopping 80% decline compared to the same period last year. Such a monumental difference of $5.83 between the two EPS calculations actually relates to pre-tax losses and additional charges of around $1.8 billion on some of the ongoing programs, which are discussed later in the article. The overall demand backlog by the end of the second quarter was $166.5 billion, slightly lower than $173 billion in the previous quarter. This decline can be attributed to uncertainties linked with the F-35 program, which is expected to finish in 2025 with up to 190 deliveries.

Taking $1.8 Billion Hit in Q2: One-Off or Recurring?

During the Q2 earnings call, CEO Jim Taiclet addressed the $1.8 billion charges that the company recorded during the second quarter. He gave assurance regarding the future by saying that such a one-time hit to de-risk these legacy programs was taken after thorough deliberations with the company's CFO. Investors reacted positively to such a cleanup, as the LMT share price rallied after the announcement. For context, the following table shows the breakdown of all losses & charges that were recorded during the second quarter.

Shifting focus back on the statements made by the CEO, one needs to look beyond the second quarter results and see if such charges and losses indicate some form of a recurring pattern in recent history. During 2024 as well, LMT recorded $2 billion losses on some of its classified programs, including a $1.4 billion charge during the final quarter on one of the MFC segment programs. This brings the total amount of losses over a course of 6 quarters to $3.8 billion, of which a larger chunk was incurred during the previous 3 quarters. This points towards an increasing skepticism among the investors lately, and also calls for a more cautious approach towards the LMT stock. The final half of 2025 will project a clearer picture of the continuation of such losses.

Cash Flow Deterioration & Payouts Sustainability

Beyond the accounting profitability metrics, LMT’s cash flow position also deteriorated during the second quarter. This was more alarming for a sector heavyweight like LMT, which has always attracted interest from investors because of its strong cash flow position. In fact, it is due to such cash flows that LMT is now just 3 years shy of becoming a dividend aristocrat, having exhibited annual dividend growth for 22 consecutive years.

During the quarter, the company used up more cash than it could generate, resulting in a negative $150 million in free cash flow. Compare this to $1.51 billion during the same period in 2024, and you get an idea of how much deterioration occurred. Management has attributed this to a high amount of cash tied up in inventory and receivables, as well as the amounts withheld by the U.S. government on account of F-35 program delays.

How much the current situation will affect LMT’s solid payout history is something to be seen in the near future. Both the dividend payout ratio of 93% and the FCF yield-to-dividend yield ratio are well below the sector medians. Hence, it won’t be surprising to see a dividend cut in the foreseeable future.

Regarding dividend cuts, another factor that should not be ignored by LMT shareholders and potential investors is the recent interest shown by the Pentagon in acquiring stakes in major defense industry players. Lockheed Martin is one of the targets, given their massive reliance on the U.S. government for high-value contracts. Many industry experts have expressed their concerns about such planned moves by the Pentagon. They fear this might lead to an innovation halt within the industry, along with unfair practices in allocating large contracts. For shareholders, there is an added risk of politically motivated decision-making that might outrank the shareholder interests.

For a moment, let’s assume that the U.S. Government proceeds to buy a stake in LMT. On one end, LMT could benefit by getting large contracts from the government, which will have voting rights. But on the flip side, the government might be adamant to continue paying hefty dividends to shareholders. One could look at it as a form of state capitalism, where long-term strategic interests of the country are prioritized ahead of shareholder gains. The government might also be more aligned towards funding the company’s large-scale projects organically, by retaining profits. Although it does not sound bad at all for common shareholders, the underlying motivations behind such retention have nothing to do with them.

Altering Revenue Mix for Growth Opportunities

Earlier in this article, I gave a breakdown of revenue contribution from LMT’s four business segments. Aerospace is the biggest driver of topline, generating approximately 40% of the total sales. At the same time, this segment was also the largest contributor to $3.8 billion in project losses and charges recorded during 2024 and 2025. Relate that to the graph presented above, and one can clearly see the segment’s ongoing woes resulting in negative operating margins during the second quarter. With looming uncertainty on the TR-3 upgrades and cost overruns on some of the underlying programs, there is a good chance that struggles for this segment will continue in the near term. There is no doubt that the aerospace segment is currently operating some integral programs that will continue to have a decent demand in the future. But it is unlikely that this segment and the underlying programs will help LMT capitalize on evolving trends within the global defense industry.

Recall from earlier in the article two of the segments, i.e., Missiles & Fire Control (MFC) and Space, contribute around 35-37% to the topline. And this is exactly where the opportunity lies for LMT. The revenue mix needs to be tilted more towards these two segments in light of futuristic developments in the global defense industry. For instance, LMT is currently a major contractor for the U.S. Space Force’s Space-Based Infrared System (SBIRS) program. The purpose of this program, which falls under the company’s Space segment, is to detect threats through satellites that have infrared detectors. In my personal opinion, LMT’s exposure to SBIRS could be a stepping stone to becoming a vital player within the futuristic Golden Dome project. The Golden Dome is a multi-layered missile shield architecture that is being compared with Israel’s Iron Dome. It will use satellite-based sensors and interceptors to detect missile threats from anywhere in the world. Beyond the Golden Dome, this segment could also benefit from the monumental growth expected in the global space economy over the next decade. As perMcKinsey forecasts earlier this year, the space segment will offer a $1.8 trillion opportunity for global economic growth by 2035.

Similarly, various programs being run under the MFC segment could pave the way for LMT to keep up with futuristic trends in defense shielding. Through programs like Next Gen Interceptor (NGI) and the Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD) missile system, the MFC segment could prove to be a bedrock in the multi-decade Golden Dome program. The segment was recently awarded a $233 million firm-fixed-price contract with the U.S. Naval Forces. It involves the delivery of 59 infrared receivers, 20 inertial measurement units, and 45 Processors for the U.S. Navy and the Air National Guard (ANG). In the latest development relevant to the MFC segment, the U.S. State Department approved a $1.75 billion sale of M142 High Mobility Artillery Rocket Systems (HIMARS) to Canada, with LMT being the primary contractor. Prior to that, the sale of an additional 48 units of M142 HIMARS to Australia was also sanctioned. This will result in sale proceeds of $705 million, and it follows the delivery of 42 units back in March 2025.

Valuation

The above chart points to a drop in share price during a single trading day, right after LMT announced the second quarter results. But the market sentiment has picked up since the instant panic witnessed on July 22nd, 2025, that saw the share price go down by almost 11% by the close of day. It is a good possibility that investors overbought on the idea that LMT management made deliberate efforts to take one-time hits on their projects to actually pave the way for future growth. But as I have already discussed above, these losses might not be one-time, and investors should not rule out the possibility of more such hits in the near future.

Despite a 22% jump in LMT share price since 22nd July, the stock has still yielded relatively lower on a YTD basis. Compared to broader S&P 500 returns of around 14%, LMT has returned just 3.52% during the first 10 months of 2025. Comparison with listed peers such as Boeing (NYSE:BA), General Dynamics (NYSE:GD), and Northrop Grumman (NYSE:NOC) tells a similar tale. Looking at the YTD returns of 30.65% for GD, 30.19% for NOC, and 25.58% for BA, one could strongly argue that LMT is trading at a very attractive discount to its peers. However, when looking at the valuation multiples, the opinion certainly changes.

LMT’s valuation multiples are enough to convince a fundamentals-driven investor to remain bearish on the stock. The most alarming ones are the price-to-book and price-to-cash-flow metrics. LMT is currently trading at a forward P/B ratio of 22.70, which is 587.31% higher than the sector median. This reflects an enormous overvaluation caused by a decline in the company’s book value per share due to the project losses recorded in recent quarters. Similarly, the TTM price-to-cash flow ratio of 22.98 also shows a 56% overvaluation relative to the sector median.

Risks

Challenges around the integration of the TR-3 update for the F-35 program might lead to further delays on the scheduled timeline. Despite the delivery of non-combat-ready jets, there is a lack of clarity on a complete upgrade that forms the basis for futuristic Block 4 capabilities. There are evident financial implications of such lags, as the costs of completion for these upgrades will mount.

As we highlighted above, LMT is in need of shuffling up its revenue mix and allocating more focus towards MFC and Space segments. Comparing the earlier-mentioned contracts awarded to these two segments with recent developments in the Aerospace and RMS divisions, it becomes evident that LMT management does not seem very ambitious to alter its revenue mix very soon. Take the example of the Rotary and Mission Systems (RMS) segment, which is the second largest contributor. LMT’s subsidiary, Sikorsky, which is a part of the RMS segment, won a $10.85 billion helicopter contract from the U.S. Navy last week. The magnitude of such deals for the RSM and Aerospace segments overshadows the other two areas, which is something that needs to change. Otherwise, the company will continue to face revenue concentration risk.

Finally, there is a risk of a major correction in the LMT stock given its inflated valuation multiples and the possibility of dividend cuts amid a cash crunch. Any such dividend cuts after an impressive track record of payouts will put off investors who might opt to liquidate their holdings in LMT.

Final Word

A section within the market might be tempted by LMT’s attractive valuations, but one should never ignore business fundamentals. Moreover, Seeking Alpha also currently has assigned a Hold rating to LMT with a 2.71 Quant Ratings score. Based on my own analysis, where I highlighted the underlying challenges for management both in the near and long term, I back this rating. I will closely monitor how certain fundamental, industrial, and political factors evolve in the coming quarters before investing in LMT. Prior to that, my stance is Hold.

data.title: Taming Style Rotation Whiplash
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

The past 18 months have delivered a masterclass in market whiplash. The growth style of U.S. equities dominated 2023 and 2024 as artificial intelligence (AI)-fueled optimism and mega-cap leadership drove strong returns, to the point of coining the term ‘Magnificent 7'1 to describe seven large companies. To make it crystal clear:

  • The S&P 500 Growth Index returned nearly twice the average annual return, 33.07% compared to 17.18%, of the S&P 500 Value Index over these two years.2

In early 2025, however, the pendulum swung violently in the opposite direction when tariff shocks and policy uncertainty triggered a broad sell-off. For a moment, value exposures looked poised for resurgence. Yet within weeks, markets lurched back to risk-on mode, restoring growth's leadership almost as quickly as it had been questioned. To put numbers to it3:

  • We all remember the shock of the initial tariff announcements in April 2025. From January 1, 2025, to April 8, 2025, the S&P 500 Value Index returned -11.49%, which was markedly better than the S&P 500 Growth Index's return of -18.20%.
  • However, extending to the year-to-date 2025 period as of September 12, 2025, we see the S&P 500 Growth Index up 17.68%, while the S&P 500 Value Index was up 7.83%.

This back-and-forth has left investors grappling with a difficult question: should they chase whichever style regime seems ascendant or attempt to forecast the next rotation? Style leadership can flip on macroeconomic headlines as much as on fundamentals, and attempts to time the turns often leave portfolios exposed at precisely the wrong moment. The April 2025 episode was instructive: investors who leaned entirely into either growth or value found themselves on the wrong side of at least part of the cycle.

That is why a blended approach, anchored in both quality growth and resilient value, has gained importance. At WisdomTree, for those seeking a core U.S. equity strategy, we have:

  • WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Fund (QGRW): QGRW is designed to track the total return performance, after fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index. The strategy focuses on large-cap U.S. equities that have proved capable of delivering strong return on equity, return on assets and earnings growth metrics.
  • WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund (WTV): WTV seeks income and capital appreciation by investing primarily in U.S. equity securities that provide a high total shareholder yield with favorable relative quality characteristics.

With a 50/50 blend of these strategies, investors can construct a core that aims to sidestep the need for perfect timing. Growth holdings capture the structural forces reshaping the economy as well as AI adoption, digital transformation and earnings momentum. At the same time, value holdings bring ballast through shareholder yield, cash flow strength and cyclical participation. Together, they create a portfolio that can help absorb rotation shocks without being defined by them.

As of September 2025, the lesson is clear: markets are rewarding adaptability, not purity. Concentrated bets on a single style may still deliver bursts of outperformance, but they come with stomach-churning volatility and frequent reversals. A core built on the complementary strengths of QGRW and WTV seeks to offer something different: a disciplined way to harness innovation while maintaining resilience through fundamentals. In an era of style rotation whiplash, balance is not a compromise, it is a strategy for durability.

Figure 1: Standardized Performance

Sources: WisdomTree, Morningstar, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 9/8/25 with returns as of 6/30/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. In the case of WTV, the Fund's objective changed effective 12/18/17. Prior to 12/18/17, Fund performance reflects the investment objective of the Fund when it tracked the performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Value Index. The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performance, click the relevant ticker: QGRW, WTV.

  • Consistent edge: Across nearly every horizon, month-to-date, quarter-to-date, one year and the longest common period, the QGRW/WTV blend either slightly outpaces or keeps pace with the S&P 500 Index, showing it doesn't need a perfect market environment to stay competitive.
  • Compounding outperformance: 2025 has felt like a unique period with the start of the Trump Administration 2.0 and the veritable firehose of policy that has come with it. It's notable that extending beyond 2025 in the one-year and longest common period time frames led to a greater difference in performance, in both cases favoring the QGRW/WTV blend.

Figure 2: The Balance that Beat the Benchmark

Sources: WisdomTree, Morningstar, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 9/14/25 with returns as of 9/12/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. The Longest Common Period is constrained by the inception of QGRW. In the case of WTV, the Fund's objective changed effective 12/18/17. Prior to 12/18/17, Fund performance reflects the investment objective of the Fund when it tracked the performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Value Index. The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performance, click the relevant ticker: QGRW, WTV.

Judging Consistency: Rolling Period

Figure 3, showing one-year rolling returns, tells a story of consistency with nuance: the 50/50 allocation to QGRW and WTV (blue line) has generally tracked the S&P 500 (black line) closely, but with a modest return advantage during most periods. Both series exhibit the same cyclical rhythm, peaking near 40%–45% rolling returns in late 2024, then dipping sharply into early 2025 before recovering steadily into mid-2025. The allocation's persistent edge, even through drawdowns, underscores how blending quality growth and value tilts can smooth performance and maintain a consistency advantage relative to the market benchmark.

Figure 3: Rolling 1-Year Returns: Consistency with an Edge over the S&P 500 Index

Sources: WisdomTree, Morningstar, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 9/14/25 with returns as of 7/31/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. The Longest Common Period is constrained by the inception of QGRW. In the case of WTV, the Fund's objective changed effective 12/18/17. Prior to 12/18/17, Fund performance reflects the investment objective of the Fund when it tracked the performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Value Index. The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performance, click the relevant ticker: QGRW, WTV.

Close Enough to Comfort, Different Enough to Matter

The allocation's sector and stock-level exposures highlight a key point: while there are notable differences compared to the S&P 500 Index, they stop short of being outsized bets. The 50/50 blend of QGRW and WTV, as seen in figure 4a, leans slightly less into Information Technology overall, with more balanced representation in areas like Consumer Discretionary and Financials. At the stock level, as seen in figure 4b, this translates into lighter weights in mega-cap names like Nvidia and Microsoft, though those companies remain core holdings. These distinctions show a conscious effort to diversify rather than concentrate risk, while still keeping the portfolio's largest drivers aligned with the benchmark.

For investors, this positioning offers familiarity without mimicry. Many market participants critique the S&P 500 for its concentration in a handful of technology giants, but they're also hesitant to deviate too far from it, since moving drastically away can trigger discomfort or tracking error concerns. The QGRW/WTV blend strikes a middle ground: recognizable sector and stock exposures that closely resemble the benchmark, but with subtle tilts that emphasize quality growth and value factors. In other words, the portfolio is differentiated enough to add potential value, yet not so different that it risks alienating benchmark-sensitive investors.

Figure 4a: Balanced Sector Positioning Relative to the Benchmark

Figure 4b: Top 10 Holdings: Similar Foundations, Subtly Different Weights

Conclusion: Balanced Positioning for Investors Who Value both Alignment and Edge

In conclusion, the 50/50 blend of QGRW and WTV delivers what many investors seek but rarely find: alignment with the comfort of the S&P 500 benchmark, paired with thoughtful, incremental differentiation. The sector and stock-level exposures show familiar leadership, anchored by the same mega-cap names, but with subtle adjustments that reduce concentration risk without introducing undue tracking error. Rolling returns underscore this balance, demonstrating a pattern of consistency with a modest performance edge. For investors, the result is a portfolio that potentially feels close enough to the benchmark to inspire confidence, yet different enough to matter in driving long-term outcomes.

1 Refers to Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Microsoft, Meta Platforms, Nvidia and Tesla.

2 Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, with data from 1/1/23–12/31/24.

3 Sources for bullets: WisdomTree, FactSet, for specified periods.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

WTV: Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. While the Fund is actively managed, the Fund’s investment process is expected to be heavily dependent on quantitative models and the models may not perform as intended.

QGRW: Growth stocks, as a group, may be out of favor with the market and underperform value stocks or the overall equity market. Growth stocks are generally more sensitive to market movements than other types of stocks. The Fund is non-diversified and, as a result, changes in the market value of a single security could cause greater fluctuations in the value of Fund shares than would occur in a diversified fund. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit. The Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets and the Index may not perform as intended.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.
